ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers spring rolls generically; no fried Vietnamese-specific sub-code available. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese fried spring roll.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Vietnamese fried spring rolls is approximately 2 pieces, weighing around 80 g, as commonly served as a starter or side dish in Vietnamese restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Vietnamese Spring Roll (Fried) — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Vietnamese spring roll (fried). Input record confidence 0.95 with HPB provenance retained.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Paper and Fried Wheat-Starch Wrappers — Analogous Estimate Based on Fried Spring Roll / Rice Paper GI Data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Vietnamese fried spring roll (chả giò) found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) estimated based on analogous fried spring roll / rice paper roll entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Deep-frying of rice paper wrappers with a mixed meat-vegetable filling is consistent with medium GI range (60–70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 19.8) / 100 ≈ 12.9, rounded to 13.0 for an 80 g serving (24.77 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 0.80 = 19.8 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 454 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg per 100 g threshold for hypertension concern, driven by fish sauce in the filling
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Vietnamese fried spring rolls to one piece per occasion, avoid dipping sauces that add further sodium, and request baked or air-fried alternatives where available to reduce both sodium and fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65) combined with high fat (20.6 g per 100 g) and refined rice paper carbohydrates (24.8 g per 100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.76 g per 100 g) creates an unfavourable glycaemic and metabolic profile
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to a maximum of two small pieces per meal, pair with a high-fibre vegetable side to blunt the glycaemic response, and prefer fresh (non-fried) Vietnamese spring rolls as a significantly lower-GI and lower-fat alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat at 20.6 g per 100 g from deep-frying, elevated sodium at 454 mg per 100 g, and energy density of 325 kcal per 100 g collectively raise cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Vietnamese fried spring rolls as an occasional food only, limiting to one piece per serving, avoiding dipping sauces, and choosing steamed or fresh spring roll variants to substantially reduce fat, sodium, and caloric burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; sodium at 454 mg per 100 g also exceeds the RA threshold of 400 mg per 100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Vietnamese fried spring rolls due to AGE formation from deep-frying and high sodium content; fresh spring rolls are strongly preferred as an anti-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vietnamese fried spring rolls, known as chả giò in southern Vietnam and nem rán in the north, are a classic Vietnamese appetiser and snack consisting of a savoury filling of minced pork, glass noodles, shredded carrot, wood ear mushroom, and seasoning wrapped tightly in thin rice paper and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is characteristically crunchy and blistered from deep-frying, while the interior is moist, savoury, and aromatic. They are typically served with nước chấm dipping sauce (a mixture of fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli) and fresh lettuce and herbs for wrapping. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 325 kcal, 10 g protein, 20.6 g fat, and 24.8 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 454 mg per 100 g driven by fish sauce. The deep-frying process significantly increases fat content and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making this food a high-concern item for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 13 per 80 g serving. Chả giò are widely available in Vietnamese restaurants, hawker centres, and school canteens across Southeast Asia, and are a culturally significant dish served at family gatherings, Tết (Lunar New Year), and festive occasions.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with input confidence 0.95 and canonical provenance. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which reduces GI sub-score confidence but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong macro provenance.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Vietnamese fried spring roll are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. Input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' which are incorrect for a pork-filled fried spring roll; these have been removed and is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false. The input 'diabetes' condition key has been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on fried spring roll / rice paper roll entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for chả giò was found. Glycemic load per 80 g serving calculated as (65 × 19.82) / 100 = 12.9, rounded to 13.0.
